content: If $4^{\text {th }}, 10^{\text {th }}$ and $16^{\text {th }}$ terms of a G.P. are $x, y$ and $z$ respectively, then
explanation:
In a G.P. the $n$th term is $a r^{\,n-1}$. Thus

4th term: $x = a r^3$

10th term: $y = a r^9$

16th term: $z = a r^{15}$

Now

$$xz = (a r^3)\,(a r^{15}) = a^2r^{18} = (a r^9)^2 = y^2$$

so

$$y^2 = xz \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y = \sqrt{xz}.$$

Answer: Option B.

content: If $S_n$ stands for sum to $n$-terms of a GP with $a$ as the first term and $r$ as the common ratio, then $S_n: S_{2 n}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & S_n=\frac{a\left(1-r^n\right)}{1-r} \\ & S_{2 n}=\frac{a\left(1-r^{2 n}\right)}{1-r} \\ & \frac{S_n}{S_{2 n}}=\frac{a\left(1-r^n\right)}{1-r} \times \frac{1-r}{a\left(1-r^{2 n}\right)}=\frac{1-r^n}{\left(1+r^n\right)\left(1-r^n\right)} \end{aligned}$$

Hence, $S_n: S_{2 n}=\frac{1}{1+r^n}$

content: If $$p\left(\frac{1}{q}+\frac{1}{r}\right), q\left(\frac{1}{r}+\frac{1}{p}\right), r\left(\frac{1}{p}+\frac{1}{q}\right)$$ are in $$\mathrm{AP}$$, then $$p, q, r$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { } p\left(\frac{1}{q}+\frac{1}{r}\right), q\left(\frac{1}{r}+\frac{1}{p}\right), r\left(\frac{1}{p}+\frac{1}{q}\right) \\ & =p\left(\frac{q+r}{q r}\right), q\left(\frac{p+r}{p r}\right), r\left(\frac{p+q}{p q}\right) \\ & =\frac{p(q+r)}{q r}+1, \frac{q(p+r)}{p r}+1, r\left(\frac{p+q}{p q}\right)+1 \quad \text{On adding 1 each term)} \end{aligned}$$

$$=\frac{p q+p r+q r}{q r}, \frac{q p+q r+p r}{p r}, \frac{r p+r q+p q}{p q}$$

On dividing $$p q+p r+q r$$ and term, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{q r}, \frac{1}{p r}, \frac{1}{p q} \text { in } \mathrm{AP} \\ & =\frac{p q r}{q r}, \frac{p q r}{p r}, \frac{p q r}{p q} \text { in APs }\quad \text{(on multiply pqr both order)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow p,q,r$$ also in AP.

We have,

$$\begin{aligned} & 1+\frac{3}{7}+\frac{5}{72}+\frac{7}{73}+\ldots \\ & \frac{1}{70}+\frac{3}{7}+\frac{5}{72}+\frac{7}{73}+\ldots \end{aligned}$$

Numerator are in AP

So, $$T_n$$ of $$\mathrm{AP}=1+(n-1)^2=2 n-1$$

Denominator are in GP

So, $$T_n$$ of $$\mathrm{GP}=7^{n-1}$$

$$T_n$$ of given series $$=\frac{2 n-1}{7^{n-1}}$$

content: If $$A=\{1,2,3, \ldots, 10\}$$, then number of subsets of $$A$$ containing only odd numbers is
explanation:
Given, $$A=\{1,2,3, \ldots, 10\}$$

Set $$A$$ containing only odd number $$=\{1,3,5,7,9\}$$

Number of subsets $$=2^5$$ [it contains empty set also]

The required number of subsets of $$A$$ containing only odd number $$=2^5-1$$

$$=32-1=31$$

content: If $$a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_{10}$$ is a geometric progression and $$\frac{a_3}{a_1}=25$$, then $$\frac{a_9}{a_5}$$ equals
explanation:
Given, GP : $$a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_{10}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \text { and } & \frac{a_3}{a_1}=25 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{a r^2}{a}=25 \Rightarrow r^2=25 \end{array}$$

Now, $$\quad \frac{a_9}{a_5}=\frac{a r^8}{a r^4}=r^4=\left(r^2\right)^2=(25)^2=5^4$$

content: If the set $$x$$ contains 7 elements and set $$y$$ contains 8 elements, then the number of bijections from $$x$$ to $$y$$ is
explanation: Number of bijections from $$x$$ to $$y$$ will be zero because only sets with the same cardinality have bijections between them and it is given that number of elements in $$x$$ and $$y$$ are not same.

content: If the middle term of the AP is 300, then the sum of its first 51 terms is
explanation:
Given, number of terms, $$n=51$$

$$\because n$$ is odd.

$$\therefore$$ Middle term will be $$\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)$$th term.

$$\begin{array}{ll} & =\left(\frac{51+1}{2}\right) \text { th term }=26 \text {th term } \\ \therefore \quad & T_{26}=300 \end{array}$$

$$a+25 d=300 \quad\left[\because T_n=a+(n-1) d\right]$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad T_1+25 d=300\quad \left[\because T_1=a\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad T_1=300-25 d \\ & \text { and } T_{51}=a+50 d=T_1+50 d=300-25 d+50 d \\ & =300+25 d \\ & \therefore S_{51}=\frac{51}{2}[300-25 d+300+25 d] \\ & \quad=\frac{51}{2}[600]=15300 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the sum of $$n$$ terms of an AP is given by $$S_n=n^2+n$$, then the common difference of the $$\mathrm{AP}$$ is
The third term of a GP is 9. The product of its first five terms is
explanation:
Let first term and common ratio of a GP are $$a$$ and $$r$$ respectively

$$\therefore \quad a r^2=9 \quad \text{..... (i)}$$

then product of first five terms is GP

$$\begin{aligned} & =(a)(a r)\left(a r^2\right)\left(a r^3\right)\left(a r^4\right) \\ & =a^5 r^{10}=\left(a r^2\right)^5=(9)^5(\text { by using (i)) } \end{aligned}$$

content: If $a, b, c$ are three consecutive terms of an AP and $x, y, z$ are three consecutive terms of a GP, then the value of $x^{b-c} \cdot y^{c-a} \cdot z^{a-b}$ is
explanation:
Given that $a$, $b$, and $c$ are three consecutive terms of an arithmetic progression (AP), and $x$, $y$, and $z$ are three consecutive terms of a geometric progression (GP), we want to find the value of the expression $x^{b-c} \cdot y^{c-a} \cdot z^{a-b}$.

Analysis:

For an AP, the difference between consecutive terms is constant. Thus, if $a$, $b$, and $c$ are in AP, we have:

$ b - a = c - b = d $

Thus, $c - a = 2d$.

For a GP, the ratio between consecutive terms is constant. Hence, if $x$, $y$, and $z$ are in GP, then:

$ \frac{y}{x} = \frac{z}{y} = r \quad \Rightarrow \quad y^2 = xz $

Now, let's simplify the expression $x^{b-c} \cdot y^{c-a} \cdot z^{a-b}$:

$ \begin{align*} x^{b-c} \cdot y^{c-a} \cdot z^{a-b} &= x^{-(c-b)} \cdot y^{2d} \cdot z^{-(b-a)} \\ &= x^{-d} \cdot y^{2d} \cdot z^{-d} \\ &= (x \cdot z)^{-d} \cdot y^{2d} \end{align*} $

Since $y^2 = xz$, it follows that:

$ y^{-2d} \cdot y^{2d} = 1 $

Therefore, the value of the expression is:

$ x^{b-c} \cdot y^{c-a} \cdot z^{a-b} = 1 $

The sequence given is:

$ S_n = 3 + 5 + 7 + \ldots $

This sequence is an arithmetic progression (AP) where:

The first term $ a = 3 $

The common difference $ d = 5 - 3 = 2 $

To find the sum of the first $ n $ terms ($ S_n $) of this AP, we use the formula for the sum of an arithmetic sequence:

$ S_n = \frac{n}{2} [2a + (n-1)d] $

Substitute the known values into the formula:

$ S_n = \frac{n}{2} [2 \times 3 + (n-1) \times 2] $

Simplify the expression:

$ = \frac{n}{2} [6 + 2n - 2] $

$ = \frac{n}{2} [2n + 4] $

$ = n(n + 2) $

Thus, the sum of the first $ n $ terms of the sequence is $ n(n+2) $.
